Chalin, J. Kinirya, G. Leavens, and E. Poll. Beyond assertions: Advanced specification and verification with JML and ESC/Java2. FMCO'05, LNCS 4111, 77–101. Springer, 2006

courses.name: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with fundamental techniques and methods in artificial intelligence and to enable them to use AI methods in solving practical problems.
courses.description: Fundamental concepts and methods of artificial intelligence. Problem solving with AI; search methods with and without problem knowledge. Local search methods and simulated annealing algorithm. Metaheuristic algorithms. Introduction to artificial neural networks. Game problems. Prolog programming language, knowledge representation, and logical inference.
learning_outcomes: Knowledge of fundamental methods in the field of artificial intelligence.
Ability to formulate and solve practical problems using fundamental methods in the field of artificial intelligence.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Basic Concepts, history and philosophy of artificial intelligence
Hafta 3: Problem Solving with Artificial Intelligence and Introduction to Search Algorithms
Hafta 4: Search Algorithms without Problem Knowledge
Hafta 12: Prolog Programming Language and Logical Programming
Hafta 13: Prolog Programming Language and Logical Programming
resources: Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach. Stuart Russell, Peter Norvig, Prentice Hall, Second Edition

courses.name: Data Science and Introduction to Big Data Analytics
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce data science and data analysis using statistical learning methods. Classical statistical methods will be combined with computational and machine learning methods and recent developments in computer science. Students are expected to gain experience in applying analytical methods on sample datasets and using data analysis techniques.
courses.description: Data Science and Big Data Analytics, relational databases and data modeling, data warehouse and integration, parallel databases, Hadoop/MapReduce/Spark, data visualization, machine learning, classification and regression, clustering, natural language processing, information retrieval, and network analysis.
learning_outcomes: Knowledge of fundamental methods in Data Science and Big Data Analytics.
Ability to model and solve practical problems using Data Science and Big Data Analytics methods.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Data Science and Big Data Analytics
Hafta 2: Relational Databases and Data Modeling
Hafta 11: Introduction to Natural Language Processing
resources: Data Science from Scratch, O’Reilly Media, Joel Grus (2015)
Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn and TensorFlow, O’Reilly Media, Aurélien Géron (2017)

courses.name: Computer Architecture
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the fundamentals of computer architecture and help them understand the roles of hardware and software at low machine levels.
courses.description: Basic principles of computer design, instruction set principles, pipelining and pipeline hazards, instruction-level parallelism, methods for avoiding pipeline hazards, static and dynamic instruction scheduling mechanisms, and memory hierarchy design.
learning_outcomes: Have sufficient knowledge of computer architecture and be able to apply theoretical and practical knowledge in this area to model and solve engineering problems.
Be able to compare and explain different architectures and organizations using various criteria.
Have sufficient knowledge of static and dynamic instruction scheduling methods and be able to apply these methods.
Have sufficient knowledge of superscalar pipelined processors, dynamic branch prediction, simultaneous multithreading, multicore processors, and memory hierarchy.
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Performance and power measurement and reporting
Hafta 3: Instruction set principles, MIPS instruction set
Hafta 4: Basic pipeline theory, multicycle operations
Hafta 12: Memory system II – main and virtual memory
resources: Hennessy, J., Patterson, D., "Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach", 5th Ed., Morgan Kaufmann

courses.name: Mobile Application Development for Mobile Systems
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with knowledge of basic mobile infrastructures and technologies, and to develop their ability to use modern mobile application design and development tools and implementation methods.
courses.description: This course introduces mobile systems, mobile operating systems and application development environments, mobile networks and communication infrastructures (GSM, CDMA, 3G/WCDMA/UMTS), short-range communication technologies (Bluetooth, WiFi), application development techniques for mobile systems (Android-Java, iOS-Swift), and mobile application publishing and distribution.
learning_outcomes: Have sufficient knowledge of mobile systems and mobile infrastructures; apply theoretical and practical knowledge in these areas to model and solve engineering problems.
Design and implement mobile applications under realistic constraints and conditions, including hierarchy design, classes, and methods.
Have knowledge and implementation ability in developing interactive software with versatile communication for mobile and portable devices such as cell phones and tablets.
Have knowledge of current technologies required for software development on mobile systems and be able to use them effectively.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to mobile computing and technologies
Hafta 2: Mobile system design and context awareness
Hafta 3: Introduction to the Android operating system
Hafta 5: Web services and access to web services
Hafta 6: Data persistence and file I/O
Hafta 7: Accessing SMS, network, and the Internet through Android
Hafta 11: Introduction to the Swift programming language
Hafta 13: Swift: graphical user interface elements
Hafta 14: Swift: simple network communication with Swift
resources: Course notes
Lab materials
Internet & World Wide Web: How to Program, by P. Deitel (3rd edition)
Programming the World Wide Web, by Robert W. Sebesta (5th edition)

courses.name: Sanal Gerçekliğe Giriş
courses.purpose: Bu ders kapsamında dokunsal sistemlere ağırlık verilmek üzere sanal gerçeklik ile ilgili farklı konular ele alınmaktadır. Ders, öğrencilerin dokunsal destekli sanal gerçeklik simülasyon ve uygulamaları geliştirmelerini amaçlamaktadır. Ayrıca öğrencilerin sanal gerçeklik uygulamaları ile ilgili temel bilgiler edinmeleri ve farklı grafik ile dokunsal etkileşim kütüphaneleriyle uygulama yapmaları hedeflenmektedir.
courses.description: Sanal gerçeklik sistemlerinin temelleri, geometrik modelleme, dönüşümler, grafik ve dokunsal imge oluşturma, uzamsal gösterimler ve dönüşümler, sanal gerçeklik sistemlerinin değerlendirilmesi.
learning_outcomes: Temel sanal gerçeklik kavramları konusunda bilgi.
Unity3D ile 3 boyutlu sanal dünyada uygulama geliştirme.
Google Cardboard, haptic cihazlar ya da derinlik sensörleri ile bir sanal gerçeklik uygulaması geliştirme deneyimi.
Kullanıcı çalışmaları ile geliştirilen sistemin değerlendirilmesi becerisi.
Son teknolojiler ve sanal gerçekliğin etik ve toplumsal etkileri konusunda tartışma kanalıyla bilgilendirilme.
Grup çalışması yetileri.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Giriş: Ders gereksinimleri ve konular. Sanal gerçekliğin tanımı ve tarihi
Hafta 2: Sanal gerçeklik teknolojileri: Algılayıcılar, görüntü cihazları, alternatif-dünya üreteci, insan duyuları, algı, sanal gerçeklik uygulamaları
Hafta 4: Uzamsal tanımlar ve dönüşümler: Açı-aks gösterimi; quaternionlar; 3B dönüşümler
Hafta 6: Perspektif dönüşümler; bakış koordinatı dönüşümleri
Hafta 8: Grafik imge oluşturma; ışın izleme; gölgeleme; yönlü yansıma dağılım fonksiyonları (BRDF); tarama; barisentrik koordinatlar
Hafta 10: Katı cisim dinamiği, çarpışmalar ve dokunsal sistemlerle etkileşim
resources: LaValle, Steven M. Virtual Reality. To be published by Cambridge University Press. [http://vr.cs.uiuc.edu/vrbookbig.pdf]
Hearn, Donald, M. Pauline Baker, and Bjarne Stroustrup. Computer Graphics with OpenGL, 3/E. Prentice-Hall, 2003.
Ming Lin and Miguel Otaduy. Haptic Rendering. A K Peters, 2008.

courses.name: Image Processing and Pattern Recognition
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to provide students with knowledge and abilities to understand image processing techniques and a first introduction to pattern recognition.
courses.description: The objective of this course is to provide the fundamental concepts on digital image processing. Visual perception, image representation, image enhancement in spatial and frequency domain, image restoration, color image processing, image compression and finally, pattern recognition are the fields that will be covered during this course. At the end of this course, the student will be able to understand how digital images are treated and will acquire a basic knowledge of image processing.
learning_outcomes: Ability to use theoretical and applied information in order to understand and implement basic algorithms of image processing.
Ability to identify, formulate, and solve problems related with the recognition of objects in a image.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction – Fields of Digital Image Processing
Hafta 2: Elements of Visual Perception – The human eye
Hafta 3: Intensity Tranformation functions. Histogram Processing.
Hafta 4: Spatial filtering I. Smoothing, Shapring, Edge detection.
Hafta 5: Spatial filtering II. Smoothing, Shapring, Edge detection.
Hafta 6: Filtering in the frequency domain I.
Hafta 7: Filtering in the frequency domain II.
resources: Raphael C. Gonzalez Richard E. Woods, Digital Image Processing, Third Edition Prentice Hall 2008
Anil K. Jain, Fundamentals of Digital Image Processing, Prentice Hall, 1989

courses.name: Embedded System Programming
courses.purpose: This practical course aims to teach embedded system structures and embedded system development/programming/debugging techniques. Through a series of laboratory applications, students gain skills in developing, programming, and debugging embedded Linux systems.
courses.description: Embedded system anatomy; why embedded Linux; microprocessor fundamentals; Linux fundamentals; RS232; terminal emulators; cross-development environment (native/target compilation); bootloaders; network service setup; kernel loading (SD card and NFS/TFTP); Linux kernel configuration/compilation and root filesystem; frame buffer and touch devices; embedded graphics, embedded graphics environments, Qt/Qt Embedded, virtual frame buffer; GPIO, sysfs; Gstreamer, Gstreamer pipelines, Gstreamer TI Plug-in; loading/unloading device drivers; web server setup; wireless network setup; laboratory work and a semester project.
learning_outcomes: Sufficient knowledge of microprocessor architectures, embedded Linux, and embedded graphics.
Ability to write Qt/Qt Embedded GUI, network, and digital multimedia applications.
Ability to debug, verify, and simulate on hardware in embedded Linux systems.
Ability to effectively use current technologies required for the development of embedded Linux systems.
Ability to work in a team.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Embedded system anatomy; why Linux? Microprocessor fundamentals; Linux fundamentals.
Hafta 3: Cross-development environment, native/target compilation, software toolchain, GDB, GDBserver, TI DVSDK.
Hafta 4: BIOS and bootloader comparison, U-Boot.
Hafta 5: Network service setup, kernel loading (SD card and NFS/TFTP).
Hafta 6: Linux kernel configuration/compilation, root filesystem.
Hafta 8: Wi-Fi setup; loading/unloading device drivers; device driver fundamentals.
Hafta 9: GPIO, sysfs, file systems, frame buffer, touch screen devices.
Hafta 10: Embedded graphics, window environments, Qt/Qt Embedded, virtual frame buffer, embedded GUI application development.
Hafta 12: Digital multimedia applications; Gstreamer/Gstreamer pipelines, Gstreamer TI Plug-in.
resources: Karim Yaghmour, Building Embedded Linux Systems, O'Reilly
Christopher Hallinan, Embedded Linux Primer, Prentice Hall Open Source Software Development Series

courses.name: Audio Processing and Recognition
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the fundamental concepts of audio processing and recognition, including sound perception, psychoacoustics, time and frequency domain analysis, and speech-related processing methods. It also introduces the basic principles needed to understand and implement audio processing algorithms.
courses.description: The course covers physical sound properties, anatomy of the ear, cognition of sound, masking, audio illusions, pitch and pitch strength, time-domain and frequency-domain analysis, discrete Fourier transform, short-time Fourier transform, filters, audio and compressed audio formats, music formats, cepstrum analysis, Mel frequencies, pitch determination, text-to-speech systems, hidden Markov models, monophonic and polyphonic melody extraction, pitch extraction, audio feature extraction, audio classification, and voice over IP technologies such as H.323, IMS, and RTP.
learning_outcomes: Ability to use theoretical and applied information in order to understand and implement algorithms of audio processing.
Ability to identify, formulate, and solve problems related with frequency and speech analysis in an audio signal.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Physical Sound Properties, Anatomy of the Ear
Hafta 3: Masking, Audio illusions, Pitch and Pitch Strength
Hafta 4: Time domain analysis, frequency domain analysis..
Hafta 5: Filters and Filter Design I
Hafta 6: Filters and Filter Design II
resources: Speech and Audio Signal Processing. Processing and Perception of Speech and Music. Ben Gold, Nelson Morgan. John Wiley & Sons. 2000
Applied Speech and Audio Processing: With Matlab Examples. Ian McLoughlin. Cambridge University Press. 2007

courses.name: Software Architecture
courses.purpose: This course introduces software architecture in terms of its concepts, principles, and techniques. It aims to teach students how to define the architecture of a software system using various techniques and how to perform different operations on it. Students will also gain experience applying the learned knowledge to a project, defining and working on the architecture of sufficiently large and complex systems, and conducting research.
courses.description: The course covers the fundamental concepts of software architecture, architectural styles, software architecture modeling, software architecture description languages, the XCD software architecture description language, visualization of software architecture, UML software design language, transforming software architecture into program code, defining software architecture according to non-functional system properties, and domain-based software architecture development.
learning_outcomes: Knowledge of what software architecture means and its advantages
Knowledge of visual techniques and UML for software architecture description
Knowledge of software architecture description languages for software architecture description
Comparison of visual techniques and software architecture description languages in software architecture description
Learning software architecture analysis
Learning the transition from software architecture to program code
Learning how software architecture is defined according to non-functional system properties
Learning domain-based software architecture development
Learning how to conduct research on software architecture and write papers
weekly_topics: Hafta 6: Introduction to XCD Architectural Description Languages-1
Hafta 7: Introduction to XCD Architectural Description Languages-2
Hafta 11: Transition from Architectural Model to Program Code
Hafta 13: Designing Software Architecture According to Non-Functional System Properties
Hafta 14: Introduction to Domain-Based Software Engineering
resources: N. Taylor, N. Medvidovic, E. M. Dashofy. 2009. Software Architecture: Foundations, Theory, and Practice. 1st edition. Wiley.
P. Clements et al., Documenting Software Architectures: Views and Beyond, 2nd ed. Addison-Wesley Professional, 2010.
Ozkaya M. (2014). A Design-by-Contract based Approach for Architectural Modelling and Analysis. Ph.D. thesis, City University London, U.K.
N. Medvidovic & R. N. Taylor. A classification and comparison framework for Software Architecture Description Languages.
L. Dobrica & E. Niemela. A survey on software architecture analysis methods. IEEE Trans. on Software Engineering, Vol. 28, No. 7, pp. 638-654, July 2002.

courses.name: Compiler Design
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with knowledge and skills in compiler design and development.
courses.description: The course explains the methods used to translate one formal language into another. It covers the steps from the scanner phase through the design and development of parsers. It also introduces semantic analysis and local and global compiler optimization. Students are expected to design a simple compiler using lex and yacc software tools.
learning_outcomes: Basic knowledge about compiler phases; ability to use automata theory and formal languages knowledge.
Ability to design and develop scanner modules for compilers.
Ability to select appropriate parsing strategies for a given case. Knowledge of alternative methods such as top-down or bottom-up parsing.
Knowledge of current technologies required for compiler design and implementation, and ability to use these technologies effectively.
weekly_topics: Hafta 2: Scanners I (regular languages, lexical properties)
Hafta 3: Scanners II (RE to NFA to DFA to minDFA to implementation)
Hafta 4: Parsers I (CFGs, parse tree, top-down parsing)
Hafta 5: Parsers II (recursive descent, predictive parsing)
Hafta 8: Parsers IV (shift-reduce, SLR, LR(k) parsing)
Hafta 13: Code generation, optimizations I (local optimizations)
resources: A.V. Aho, M.S. Lam, R. Sethi, J.D. Ullman, “Compilers: Principles, Techniques and Tools, 2nd Ed., Addison Wesley, 2006.
K.D. Cooper, L. Torczon, “Engineering a Compiler”, 2nd Ed., Morgan Kaufmann, 2012.
Presentation slides on Coadsys

courses.name: Internet Teknolojileri
courses.purpose: Bu dersin amacı, öğrencilerin modern ve güncel İnternet geliştirme teknolojilerini kullanarak kullanıcı ve sunucu taraflı yazılım geliştirme konularında bilgi ve becerilere sahip olmasını sağlamaktır.
courses.description: Bu ders kapsamında İnternet çekirdek yapılarının – HTTP, DNS, TCP ve Web sunucu IP – neler olduğu ve ne şekilde hizmet verdiği detaylandırılır. Bunlara bağlı olarak kullanıcı ve sunucu taraflı teknolojiler (HTML 4.0 ve 5.0, CSS, Dinamik HTML, formlar, JavaScript, Java Applets, Perl, XML, PHP, ASP, JSP, veritabanları ve web servisler) ile internet uygulama geliştirmeye giriş yapılır.
learning_outcomes: İnternet teknolojileri konusunda yeterli bilgi birikimi; bu alanlardaki kuramsal ve uygulamalı bilgileri mühendislik problemlerini modelleme ve çözme için uygulayabilme.
Hem kullanıcı hem de sunucu tarafında internet yazılımlarını gerçekçi kısıtlar ve koşullar altında tasarlayabilme ve gerçekleştirebilme.

Gereksinimlere uygun şekilde gelişim bileşenlerinin neler olabileceğini tespit edebilme, test edebilme, kullanabilme ve karşılaştırabilme; bu alandaki kullanılmayan eski teknolojiler hakkında bilgi sahibi olma.
Programlama dilleri tasarımı ve gerçeklenmesi için gerekli olan güncel teknolojileri etkin bir şekilde kullanabilme.
weekly_topics: Hafta 10: PHP: DİZGİ İŞLEME VE RASYONEL İFADELER
Hafta 11: PHP: FORM İŞLEME VE İŞ MANTIĞI
Hafta 14: JSP VE PERL İLE SUNUCU TARAFLI KODYAZIMI
resources: Ders Notları: http://cse.yeditepe.edu.tr/v2/en/academic/course-pages
Lab malzemesi: http://cse.yeditepe.edu.tr/v2/en/academic/course-pages
INTERNET & WORLD WIDE WEB HOW TO PROGRAM, BY P. DEITEL (3RD EDITION)
PROGRAMMING THE WORLD WIDE WEB, BY ROBERT W. SEBESTA (5TH EDITION)

courses.name: Concurrent Programming
courses.purpose: This course aims to equip students with the techniques needed to write efficient concurrent programs and to solve the problems that may arise during this process.
courses.description: Multithreaded programming concepts, processes, threads, synchronization, solving safety and liveness problems, concurrent data structures.
learning_outcomes: Basic knowledge about processes, threads, multithreading concepts, and synchronization principles.
Ability to write concurrent object-oriented programs.
Ability to understand, analyze, and solve safety problems that may arise in concurrent programs.
Ability to understand, analyze, and solve liveness problems that may arise in concurrent programs.
Ability to use concurrency libraries effectively.
Ability to design and use concurrent data structures.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Concurrency Concepts: Processes and threads
Hafta 4: Safety: Safe Objects, Immutable Objects
Hafta 5: Safety: Full Concurrency, Partial Concurrency
Hafta 8: Liveness: Removing or Spreading Concurrency
Hafta 9: Java Concurrency Package: Executor, Thread Pool
Hafta 10: Java Concurrency Package: Queue, Concurrent Collections
resources: Course Notes: http://cse.yeditepe.edu.tr/v2/en/academic/course-pages
Concurrent Programming in Java, Doug Lea

courses.name: Introduction to Automotive Networks
courses.purpose: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to automotive multiplexed network buses by covering automotive principles, components, application issues, applications, and automotive communication systems, as well as smart and connected vehicle and Internet of Things architectures.
courses.description: The course examines the CAN protocol in detail, including physical layers, compatibility issues, hardware and software tools, and application layers. It also provides detailed coverage of the LIN bus, its developments, features, problems, and solutions. The course studies current and emerging network buses such as TTCAN, FlexRay, and X-by-Wire. It explains how Fail-Safe-System base chips (SBC) and other gateways are designed and built, and explores the possibilities of connecting various buses. It also analyzes wired and wireless internal and external serial connections including Safe-by-Wire plus, I2C, Media Oriented Systems Transport (MOST), remote keyless entry, tire pressure monitoring systems (TPMS), and Bluetooth.
learning_outcomes: Sufficient knowledge about automotive networks
Ability to design, select, and use modern techniques and tools required for automotive electronic systems
Individual work
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: CAN bus, bus access concepts and arbitration
Hafta 3: Time-triggered protocols – FlexRay, TTCAN, X-by-Wire
Hafta 5: Fail-safe SBCs: multiple aspects of use and reasons
Hafta 9: Intelligent transportation, connected vehicle evolution
resources: Multiplexed Networks for Embedded Systems: CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Safe-by-Wire… / Dominique Paret, Roderick Riesco / Wiley / ISBN: 978-0-470-51170-1
Vehicular Communications and Networks: Architectures, Protocols, Operation and Deployment / Wai Chen / Elsevier / ISBN: 9781782422167

courses.name: Computer Graphics
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with introductory knowledge and skills in the theory and practice of computer graphics. It covers the fundamental topics of computer graphics, mathematical principles, algorithms, and data structures, while also developing practical skills related to the applied aspects of the subject.
courses.description: Applications of computer graphics, line and curve drawing, polygon drawing, polygon filling, transformations (translation, rotation, scaling, reflection, shearing), 2-D viewing, line clipping, polygon clipping, 3-D viewing, parallel and perspective projection, 3D clipping, visible surface detection, lighting, polygon rendering, ray tracing, OpenGL.
learning_outcomes: Knowledge of computer graphics topics such as drawing, transformations, viewing, lighting, and rendering.
Ability to design computer graphics software under realistic constraints and conditions by using theoretical and practical knowledge in these areas.
Ability to find, select, and use modern tools and techniques required to design and implement computer graphics environments.
Ability to work effectively in individual and discipline-specific teams.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: INTRODUCTION (computer graphics hardware, application areas)
Hafta 4: 2D transformations (translation, rotation, scaling, reflection, shearing)
resources: Hearn, M.P. Baker, “Computer Graphics with OpenGL”, Pearson
F.S. Hill, “Computer Graphics using OpenGL”, Prentice-Hall
J.D. Foley, A. van Dam, S.K.Feiner, J.F.Hughes, R.L. Phillips, “Introduction to Computer Graphic”, Addison-Wesley

courses.name: Human Computer Interaction
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with knowledge of how human-computer interaction techniques help individuals and organizations solve real-life design and usability problems, and to develop skills for addressing such problems.
courses.description: This course introduces the fundamental pillars of the interdisciplinary field of human-computer interaction, including the human and the limits of machines. Students learn the rules that should be followed in computer systems developed with these limits in mind, as well as the models and examples that should be used. The course also covers practical methods for measuring and evaluating the usability and ease of use of designed systems.
learning_outcomes: Sufficient knowledge of internet technologies; ability to apply theoretical and practical knowledge in these areas to model and solve engineering problems.
Ability to design and implement internet software for both client and server sides under realistic constraints and conditions.
Ability to identify, test, use, and compare development components according to requirements, and knowledge of obsolete technologies in this field.
Ability to effectively use current technologies required for the design and implementation of programming languages.
weekly_topics: Hafta 11: HCI in the Software Process
Hafta 13: Collaboration and Social Media Participation
Hafta 14: Ubiquitous Computing and Augmented Reality
resources: HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION, by A. Dix, J. Finlay, G. D. Abowd, R. Beale (3rd edition), ISBN: 978-0130461094
Course notes
Lab materials
